coredata.dc:title: Ultra-sensitive molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor for patulin detection based on a novel assembling strategy using Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs
coredata.dc:creator: Hatamluyi, Behnaz
Rezayi, Majid
Beheshti, Hamed Reza
Boroushaki, Mohammad Taher
coredata.dc:description: This study introduces an innovative procedure for the development of a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor for ultra-sensitive and selective detection of patulin. Firstly, the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was decorated by nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and AuNPs-functionalized Cu-metal organic framework (Au@Cu-MOF) and then a layer of molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was grown on Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE by electropolymerization. Electrochemical techniques were used to characterize and study the electrochemical behavior of the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE, which exhibited a stable reference peak of Au@Cu-MOF at −0.11 V after elution of patulin molecules. This peak current density decreased with rebinding of patulin molecules therefore it was considered as indicator signal. The designed MIP sensor presented a wide linear range from 0.001 to 70.0 ng mL−1, and a low detection limit (0.0007 ng mL−1). This newly developed method based on the synergistic effects of N-GQDs and Au@Cu-MOF combined with MIP technique offered outstanding selectivity, sensitivity, stability, and reproducibility. The good accuracy (recovery%, 97.6–99.4) and high precision (RSD%, 1.23–4.61) of this sensing system for analysis of apple juices proved the high potential of it for rapid and low cost determination of patulin compared to chromatographic methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Patulin
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Metal-organic framework
Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization studies of N-GQDs and Au@Cu-MOF
Voltammetric behavior of different modified electrodes
Optimization of the MIP sensor fabrication and determination conditions
Optimization of parameters affecting the MIP preparation
Optimization of parameters affecting the sensor performance
Analytical performance of the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE for quantification of patulin
Comparison of performance of the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE and MIP/N-GQDs/GCE
Detection of patulin in control samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic preparation of MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-DGQs/GCE and electrochemical detection of Patulin.

(A) TEM image, (B) FTIR spectra, and (C) UV–Vis spectrum of N-GQDs, (D) TEM image, and (E) FESEM image of Cu-MOF, (F) XRD patterns of Cu-MOF and Au@Cu-MOF, (G) TEM image of Au@Cu-MOF, (H) EDX image of Au@Cu-MOF, (I) BET analyses of Cu-MOF and Au@Cu-MOF.

(A) CVs of different modified electrodes in solution of 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl, (a) bare GCE, (b) N-GQDs/GCE, (c) Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE, (d) MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE before and (e) after removing the patulin, (f) MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE after 180 s incubation in 1.0 ng mL-1 patulin solution, and (g) NIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE after removing the patulin, (B) DPVs of different modified electrodes in solution of 0.1 mol L-1 PBS, (a) bare GCE, (b) N-GQDs/GCE, (c) Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE, (d) MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE before and (e) after removing the patulin, (f) MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE after 180 s incubation in 1.0 ng mL-1 patulin solution. (C) FESEM images of bare GCE, (D) Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE, (E) MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE before and (F) MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE after removing the patulin.

Optimization of factors affecting the performance of the MIP electrode, (A) DPV measurements of MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE immersed in 2.0 mol L −1 H2SO4 for patulin removal at different times (from 0 to 14 min). (B) DPV measurements of MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE immersed in 1.0 ng mL−1 patulin solution for target rebinding at different times (from 0 to 210 s). (C) DPV measurements in PBS solutions (0.1 mol L–1) with the pH values in the range of 4.0–7.0.

(A) DPV measurements of MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE and MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/GCE in PBS solution (0.1 mol L–1, pH 7.0) containing different concentrations (0, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, 50.0, 60.0, 70.0 ng mL–1) of patulin. (B) Linear relationships of the current difference (Δi) and logarithm concentration of patulin for the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE (blue line) and MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/GCE (red line).

(A) DPV measurements of MIP/N-GQDs/GCE in 5.0 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6] 3−/4− containing 0.1 mol L−1 KCl containing different concentrations (0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0 ng mL–1) of patulin. (B) Linear relationships of the current difference (Δi) and logarithm concentration of patulin for the MIP/N-GQDs/GCE.

(A) The selectivity of the imprinted and non-imprinted sensors for patulin and different interferences. (B) Selectivity evaluation of the proposed MIP sensor for patulin (1.0 ng mL−1), 5-HMF (10.0 ng mL−1), AOH (50.0 ng mL−1), OTA (50 ng mL−1), AFB1 (50 ng mL−1) and Mixture.
Comparison of the novel patulin detection method with other modified electrode methods for the determination of patulin.
Determination of patulin in apple juice sample (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Ultra-sensitive molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor for patulin detection based on a novel assembling strategy using Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Pharmacology, Faculty of Medicine, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces an innovative procedure for the development of a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor for ultra-sensitive and selective detection of patulin. Firstly, the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was decorated by nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and AuNPs-functionalized Cu-metal organic framework (Au@Cu-MOF) and then a layer of molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was grown on Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE by electropolymerization. Electrochemical techniques were used to characterize and study the electrochemical behavior of the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE, which exhibited a stable reference peak of Au@Cu-MOF at −0.11 V after elution of patulin molecules. This peak current density decreased with rebinding of patulin molecules therefore it was considered as indicator signal. The designed MIP sensor presented a wide linear range from 0.001 to 70.0 ng mL−1, and a low detection limit (0.0007 ng mL−1). This newly developed method based on the synergistic effects of N-GQDs and Au@Cu-MOF combined with MIP technique offered outstanding selectivity, sensitivity, stability, and reproducibility. The good accuracy (recovery%, 97.6–99.4) and high precision (RSD%, 1.23–4.61) of this sensing system for analysis of apple juices proved the high potential of it for rapid and low cost determination of patulin compared to chromatographic methods.

ce:section.ce:para: Today, food safety and its significant influence on human and animal health as well as on domestic and international economy and trade is a global challenge. Surveillance studies show that patulin is one of the most important fruit toxins and its contamination in food is a great problem. Patulin is a toxic secondary metabolite produced by certain species of fungi such as Byssochlamys, Penicillium and Aspergillus [1,2]. It is usually found in many rotten cereals, fruits, and vegetables, but the highest content of patulin (∼17.5 mg/kg) was found in apple and their processed products [3,4]. Patulin is highly soluble in water and the aquatic environments are easily contaminated by it. Moreover, due to its acid resistance and thermal stability, patulin not completely eliminated from food products after the manufacturing process [5,6]. Many clinical trials demonstrate that patulin has neurotoxic, mutagenic, teratogenic and immunosuppressive effects and at high concentrations may impair the activity of some important enzymes and also cause DNA damage [2,7]. Accordingly, many international agencies have set rules to control patulin concentration at the permissible level. The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends that the maximum daily concentration of patulin in fruits and fruit juices should be less than 0.4 μg kg−1 body weight [8]. The European Union (EU) has set the maximum permitted concentration of patulin in infant fruit juice at 10 μg kg−1 and in non-infant fruit juice at 50 μg kg-1 [9]. Given its low threshold and high toxicity, quantitative determination of patulin requires very sensitive and selective analytical techniques. Accurate and reliable determination methods based on different chromatographic systems [10–14] have been successfully used for qualitative and quantitative determination of patulin. But they are generally very expensive and time consuming, require expert-user and large equipment [15].

Other detection procedures have also been used for patulin determination, such as immunoassays [16,17], spectrophotometry [18], and electrochemical techniques [19,20]. Immunoassays are sensitive techniques but they require antibodies that are expensive, difficult to find and less reproducible [21]. Although the spectrophotometry techniques are simple, fast, and inexpensive, the lack of selectivity limits their application. Electrochemical methods provide sensitive platforms for simple, rapid, inexpensive and selective determination of contaminant residues in foodstuffs [22,23]. In particular, molecularly imprinted polymer-based sensors (MIP) have been extensively employed in developing smart miniaturized equipment duo to their high specific molecular recognition and adsorption ability for the template molecules and high chemical stability [24].

Among the different methods for MIP preparation, including thermal, optical, and in-situ electrochemical polymerization, the latter is widely used as a promising method for surface imprinting due to its easy and fast execution, easy control of polymer film thickness, good adhesion to the electrode surface, and high reproducibility [25]. However, MIP sensors suffer from low sensitivity due to the limited binding sites, slow rebinding/desorbing rate, and poor conductivity of the polymeric network [26,27]. Consequently, it is important to improve the sensitivity and performance of MIP sensors for quantitative analysis of analytes. Several studies have shown that the use of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), crystalline microporous materials, effectively increased the total surface area of MIP film due to their unique structure and properties. Thanks to MOFs advantages of ultra-high porosity, stable framework, non-toxic nature, large internal surface area, and chemical functionality, the MIPs combined with MOFs presented higher binding capacity and faster rebinding kinetics [28,29]. Among them, Cu-MOFs have proven to be an ideal candidate, serving as a probe signal with superior catalytic activity, unique electric conductivity, and well-defined configuration [30–32]. The performance of these MIP-based electrochemical sensors is further enhanced when they are synthesized on the surfaces of nanostructures. N-GQDs, a significant class of carbon nanomaterial with sizes less than 10 nm and doped with nitrogen atoms, can serve as an ideal electron mediate because of its edge effects, unique quantum confinement, high conductivity, low toxicity, electrocatalytic activity, and biocompatibility [33,34]. Several reports have shown that the use of N-GQDs as modifier material has led to the development of sensitive MIP sensors with enhanced electrochemical properties [35,36].

Our objective in this study is to fabricate an efficient molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor for the ultra-sensitive detection of patulin. The N-GQDs and Au@Cu-MOF, which show great potential for sensor applications, were utilized to modify the GCE surface. Au@Cu-MOF not only provided enhanced surface area for MIP film but also was used as an electroactive species for electroanalytical measurements. The MIP film was formed on Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE by electropolymerization using aniline as functional monomer and patulin as template. By measuring the peak current of Au@Cu-MOF as response signal, the designed MIP senor for patulin detection presented a wide linear range, low detection limit, acceptable selectivity, sensitivity and stability as well as good accuracy and high precision. However, a strategy that uses Au@Cu-MOF as an electroactive material to produce analytical signal in the field of MIP sensors is unprecedented, and this research can serve as helpful basis for further advancement of MIP electrochemical sensors.
Detailed information regarding the apparatus and reagents are described in the Supporting Information.

The N-GQDs were synthesized following a simple and one-step hydrothermal process [37]. Briefly, 0.144 g of CA and 0. 015 g of urea were dissolved in 50 mL of double distilled water. Then, the mixture was sealed in an autoclave for hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 7 h. The target product was collected by centrifugation (10,000 rpm for 30 min), followed by the addition of ethanol.

The Cu-MOF was synthesized according to the previously reported procedure [31]. First, PVP (0.25 g) was dissolved in a mixed solution containing ethanol (5 mL) and DMF (5 mL). Next, a mixture of Cu(NO3)2 (22.65 mg), NH2−BDC (6.80 mg), and DMF (5 mL) was added to the above solution with ultrasonication for 20 min. This mixture was poured to a Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 100 °C for 6 h. To remove the unreacted reagents, the obtained precipitate was dissolved in 20 mL of DMF and further hydrothermally treated in an autoclave at 100 °C for 8 h. The final product was collected by centrifugation (5000 rpm, 10 min). To end, the Cu-MOF was redispersed in 2 mL of double distilled water and used for further experiments.

Au@Cu-MOF was prepared by adding HAuCl4 (20 μL, 1 %) and NaBH4 (2 mL, 2 mmol L−1) into the above Cu-MOF solution under continuous stirring for 60 min. Lastly, the resultant product was obtained by centrifugation, washed with double distilled water and dried at 60 °C.

In order to improve the electron transport performance of the electrode, the freshly polished GCE (3 mm in diameter) was first decorated with N-GQDs (10 μL, 2.0 mg mL−1, dispersed in DMF) and dried at room temperature. Then the synthesized Au@Cu-MOF (15 μL, 5.0 mg mL−1, dispersed in DMF) was coated onto its surface and dried in air to achieve Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE. To fabricate polymer membrane, 20 mmol L−1 patulin as template, 30 mmol L−1 aniline as functional monomer and 2.0 mg mL−1 Au@Cu-MOF were added to 5 mL phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.0) and electropolymerization was done by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in the potential window between 0.0 and +1.4 V with 6 cyclic scans and a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. Finally, the resultant polymer modified electrode was immersed in 2.0 mol L−1 H2SO4 solution with stirring for 8 min to remove the template molecules. For control experiments, the non-imprinted polymer (NIP) coated electrode (NIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE) was also fabricated in the similar method, without addition of patulin molecules. MIP/N-GQDs/GCE and MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/GCE was also fabricated for the comparison studies. The construction process of patulin imprinted electrode (MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE) was shown on Scheme 1 .

The results of experiments showed that after removal of the patulin molecules, the reduction peak of Cu-MOF was appeared at −0.11 V in PBS (0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.0) by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method. While, after the rebinding patulin molecules, the current density of this peak was decreased. Therefore, Au@Cu-MOF was not only used as a catalyst but also acted as electroactive material for the signal indicator. Accordingly, for electrochemical measurement of patulin, the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE was immersed in different concentrations of patulin solution for 180 s under low stirring rates to allow full patulin adsorption. Then, DPV was successively applied to record the electrode response in PBS in the potential range from−0.4 to +0.2 V with the scan rate, modulation amplitude, modulation time and step potential of 50 mV s−1, 0.05 s, 25 mV and 5 mV, respectively. The current difference (Δi = i0 – i) was considered as the response signal, which i0 is the peak height of Au@Cu-MOF after template removal and i represents the peak height of its after patulin rebinding. After each test, the electrode was stirred in 2.0 mol L−1 H2SO4 solution to remove the template molecules and re-use the electrode. To study the interface properties of different modified electrodes, CV runs were conducted in the potential range of −0.4 to +0.6 V in a solution containing KCl (0.1 mol L-1) and [Fe(CN)6]-3/-4 (1.0 mmol L-1) as probe.

Experimental design, based on a standard orthogonal array and statistical treatment of the results were performed using Minitab software version 17. For accuracy evaluation, one sample t-tests was applied using SPSS software version 20.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization studies of N-GQDs and Au@Cu-MOF
ce:section.ce:para: TEM, FT-IR and UV–vis techniques were applied to study the average diameter, uniform size distribution and chemical structure of N-GQDs. TEM image of N-GQDs shows that the N-GQDs were successfully synthesized with high dispersion without apparent aggregation. Their average diameter was about 5 nm (Fig. 1 A).

The FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs provided good evidence for the existence of functional groups in N-GQDs. In Fig. 1B, the peaks at around 3434 cm−1, 2950 cm−1, 1721 cm−1, 1615 cm−1, 1420 cm−1 and 1071 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH, NH, CO, CC, CN and CO [38,39], respectively, which revealed the presence of oxygenated functional groups, nitrogen containing groups and nitrogen doping in the N-GQDs.

In the UV–vis spectrum of N-GQDs (Fig. 1C), two strong peaks at 230 nm and 331 nm are related to the π→π* transition of CC bonds and n→π* transition of CO bonds [40,41] and one weak peak at 460 nm is related to n→π* transition of CN bonds [42,43].

The morphology of produced Cu-MOF and Au@Cu-MOF was investigated by TEM and FESEM analysis. Fig. 1D and E show that the Cu-MOF has a nearly crystalline hexagonal structure with size varying from 80 to 120 nm. Fig. 1G reveals that after the formation of AuNPs, the Cu-MOFs became almost agglomerated and their surface is surrounded by fine AuNPs.

XRD was also employed to further characterize the Cu-MOF and Au@Cu-MOF (Fig. 1F). The crystalline nature of Cu-MOF is confirmed by the sharp diffraction peaks appearing in the XRD spectrum of it. The diffraction peaks at 2θ ≈ 29.6°, 36.5°, 42.4°, 61.5°, 73.7° and 77.5°, respectively, correspond to Cu2O crystal planes (110), (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222), as well as the diffraction peaks at 2θ ≈ 43.3° and 50.4°, respectively, correspond to the Cu crystal plans (111) and (200) [44,45]. The XRD Au@Cu-MOF spectrum, in addition to the Cu2O and Cu diffraction peaks, contains the (111), (200), (220) and (311) AuNPs crystal planes which located at 2θ of 38.5°, 44.5°, 64.8° and 77.8° [32].
The EDX analysis result (Fig. 1H), indicates the presence of C, O, Cu and Au elements in the Au@Cu-MOF and confirms the chemical composition of it.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption analysis was also carried out to determine the BET specific surface area of Cu-MOF and Au@Cu-MOF. As expected, Au@Cu-MOF presented a higher surface area of 345.01 m2 g−1 than Cu-MOF (134.33 m2 g−1) (Fig. 1I) due to the presence of AuNPs in Au@Cu-MOF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Voltammetric behavior of different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the electron transfer characteristics and performance of different modified electrodes, CV measurements were conducted in a solution containing KCl (0.1 mol L−1) and [Fe(CN)6]-3/-4 (1.0 mmol L−1) as electrochemical probe after each modification step. The CVs of different electrodes were shown in Fig. 2 A. Compared to the bare GCE (curve a), the response current values on N-GQDs/GCE (curve b) was increased because of the excellent electrical conductivity and high surface area of N-GQDs. After decorating Au@Cu-MOF on the N-GQDs/GCE, obviously increased signals were observed at Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE based on synergistic effects between N-GQDs and Au@Cu-MOF (curve c). After electropolymerization and before template removal, the peak currents greatly decreased and the curve became almost a straight line (curve d), indicating the compact polymer film blocked the electrode surface and prevented the transfer of electrons. While, after the removal of patulin molecules a significant increase was observed in the response signals (curve e). This increase proves that by removing the template molecules, cavities are formed on the MIP film that allow the penetration of ferrocyanide ions to the electrode surface. When the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE was immersed in the 1.0 ng mL-1 patulin solution for 180 s, the peak current decreased again because of patulin molecules recombined with the imprinted cavities (curve f). Finally, as expected, no current response was observed with the NIP electrode (curve g).

In another experiments, the comparison of effective surface area of different electrodes was performed. The CVs for bare GCE and Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE at different scan rates (10 mV s−1 to 250 mV s−1) were shown in Fig. S1. It was observed that, both anodic and cathodic peak currents increased linearly with the square root of the scan rates (Fig. S1A, B). The experimental data illustrated the electrocatalytic reaction on these electrodes were a diffusion-controlled process [46]. Based on the Randles–Sevcik equation [47], the effective surface area for bare GCE and Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE were calculated to be ∼ 0.17 cm2 and ∼0.76 cm2, respectively.

To study the feasibility of the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor for patulin detection, DPV measurements were performed in a series of control experiments. For this purpose, DPVs were monitored in PBS in the potential range between −0.4 to +0.2 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. 2B, no peak was observed when the bare GCE and N-GQDs/GCE were used (curve a and b) due to a lack of redox mediators, while after the decoration of Au@Cu-MOF on N-GQDs/GCE, a strong peak appeared at -0.11 V (curve c) which attributed to the transformation of Cu (II) to Cu (I) and Cu [32,48]. With successive formation of MIP film, the peak current of Au@Cu-MOF was greatly decreased, meaning that the MIP film presented blocking effect to electron transfer (curve d). However, the removal of the patulin molecules resulted in the formation of channels and a significant increase in the peak current (curve e). After the rebinding process in a solution of 1.0 ng mL−1 patulin, a decrease was observed in the peak current of probe, certifying the imprinted cavities were able to recognize the patulin molecules. These results revealed that the Au@Cu-MOF can be served as a desirable indicator for monitoring electrode surface reactions.

The morphological characterization of the bare GCE, Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE and MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE before and after patulin extraction was studied by FESEM. The FESEM image of the unmodified electrode presented a smooth surface (Fig. 2C). In Fig. 2D, the Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE displayed a completely different morphology and proved that Au@Cu-MOF was attached to the electrode surface. The MIP modified electrode showed a rigid surface before exiting the templet molecules (Fig. 2E). In contrast, the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE after the extraction process revealed a porous and rough structure (Fig. 2F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the MIP sensor fabrication and determination conditions
ce:section.ce:para: A series of preliminary experiments showed that in order to achieve a highly efficient sensor for sensitive and selective detection of patulin, it is necessary to optimize important parameters that affect the MIP film preparation and analytical performance of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters affecting the MIP preparation
ce:section.ce:para: In the preparation of MIP film, important factors such as the amount of N-GQDs and the amount of Au@Cu-MOF in the Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE fabrication step also the mass ratio of patulin to Au@Cu-MOF, the molar ratio of template to monomer, and the number of CV cycles in the electropolymerization step were optimized in five levels via the standard orthogonal array design using L25 (56) matrix (Table S1) [49,50]. In this experiments, the change in the peak current of Au@Cu-MOF (Δi) in the PBS after immersing of the electrodes in 1.0 ng mL−1 patulin solution was considered as the response. The standard orthogonal array L25, the average responses of each experiment (n = 3), and the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N), are listed in Table S2. Based on the reported delta values in Table S3, the amount of Au@Cu-MOF were the most significant factor among the investigated factors. This could be due to the unique porous architecture of the Au@Cu-MOF, which provides enlarged surface area for MIP film and enhances electron transfer rate at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Furthermore, the contributions of the investigated factors on MIP efficiency were further evaluated by the analysis of variance (ANOVA) method. The ANOVA analysis results which summarized in Table S4 confirmed the results of Table S3 and the validity of the experimental design. Fig. S2 illustrated the most favorable conditions for the MIP film preparation as follows: 10 μL of N-GQDs and 15 μL of Au@Cu-MOF in the fabrication step of Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE, 1:0.75 for the mass ratio of patulin to Au@Cu-MOF, 2:3 for molar ratio of template to monomer, and 6 scan cycles in the electropolymerization step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters affecting the sensor performance
ce:section.ce:para: Selection of optimal conditions for removal of template molecules (suitable solvent and elution time) is regarded as an important factor for the formation of structurally-related binding sites in the MIP film. In this study, H2SO4 was used as the elution reagent [19,20]. The MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE was immersed in 2.0 mol L−1 H2SO4 solution under mild stirring conditions over a period of 2–14 min (Fig. 3 A). It was found that the peak current reached the maximum value after 8 min and remained constant thereafter, accordingly, the period of 8 min was selected as the best elution time.

To improve the sensitivity of the sensor, the rebinding time was also carefully studied in 1.0 ng mL−1 patulin solution for various durations. Fig. 3B exhibited that the peak current was decreased with an enhancement in the incubation time. By 180 s, it reached the minimum value and remained stable, indicating the fast binding kinetics of the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE. Therefore, 180 s was set as the optimal incubation time.

The effect of pH of patulin solution on the template molecule rebinding at the surface of MIP film was studied in the range of 4.0–7.0 using different PBS solutions. According to the results presented in Fig. 3C, the peak current decreased up to pH 6.0. After pH 6.0, it reduced again. Consequently, the optimum pH of patulin solution was set at 6.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE for quantification of patulin
ce:section.ce:para: To ascertain the applicability of the developed MIP sensor for quantitative analysis of patulin, the performance of this electrode toward various concentrations of patulin was investigated by DPV measurements under the optimum operating conditions. As presented in Fig. 4 A, the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE response was decreased with the increasing concentration of patulin, indicating the imprinted cavities have been occupied by the patulin molecules. A linear relationship was found between the peak current change (Δi) and the logarithm of patulin concentration in the range of 0.001–70.0 ng mL−1 with calibration equation of: Δi (μA) = 15.17 log Cpatulin (ng mL−1) + 65.553 (R2 = 0.9963) (Fig. 4B). The limit of detection (LOD, based on 3 Sb/m) [51] and limit of quantification (LOQ, based on 10 Sb/m) of proposed method was obtained 0.0007 and 0.0023 ng mL−1, respectively. Likewise, the same procedure was performed on MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/GCE to illustrate the influence of the N-GQDs. It was observed that compared with the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE, the Δi of the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/GCE was very weak (Fig. 4A, B) also it presented low linear range because the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE benefits from the synergistic effects of N-GQDs and Au@Cu-MOF, consequently with adopting the tactic of N-GQDs modification, the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor presents a sensitive and large-scale detection range. This novel imprinted sensor was compared with other previous reported sensors for patulin detection. As can be seen in Table 1 , the linear response range of MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE for patulin is wider than many other detection methods mentioned in the literature [5,20,52,55], as well as the obtained LOD is generally lower.

Although few detection techniques displayed advantageous linear range or detection limit, it was evident from Table 1 that the performance of proposed MIP sensor is quite comparable or even better than the previously reported methods. MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE is fast, very sensitive, low cost, and simple without any difficult pretreatment. Thus this novel sensing technique can be improved and expanded to detect the other important analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of performance of the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE and MIP/N-GQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the effect of the presence of Au@Cu-MOF on the sensing performance of the proposed sensor, the response of the MIP sensor without Au@Cu-MOF (MIP/N-GQDs/GCE) was investigated in [Fe(CN)6]−3/−4 (5.0 mmol L-1) containing KCl (0.1 mol L-1) as electrochemical probe. For this purpose, the MIP/N-GQDs/GCE was fabricated under the conditions similar to the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE (under the optimized conditions). After template removal, MIP/N-GQDs/GCE was placed in different concentrations of patulin solution for 180 s with stirring and determination of patulin was performed by DPV technique in the potential range between −0.2 to +0.6 V. As the results shown in Fig. 5 A, the peak current decreased with the raising of the patulin concentration. The calibration plot was obtained to be linear between 0.01 and 20.0 ng mL-1 and obeyed the following relation: Δi (μA) = 9.20 log Cpatulin (ng mL−1) + 22.83 (R2 = 0.9917) (Fig. 5B). Furthermore, the LOD was calculated as 0.0082 ng mL-1 (based on 3 Sb/m). Comparative studies between two MIP electrodes revealed that the linear range and sensitivity of MIP/N-GQDs/GCE was lower than that of MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE. In addition, the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE presented a lower detection limit than the MIP/N-GQDs/GCE. This is due to the fact that the combination of Cu-MOF with AuNPs provides a highly conductive framework with many specific binding sites. In this polymeric network, the analyte binding capacity has been increased, the rebinding kinetic accelerated, and the electron transfer process improved. Consequently, the introduction of the Au@Cu-MOF in MIP field can greatly improve the robustness and performance of the electrochemical sensors.

The selective detection of patulin on the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE was investigated in the presence of compounds with similar structure such as 5-hydroxymethyl-furfural (5-HMF) and other probable interfering species existing in fruit juices such as alternariol (AOH), ochratoxin A (OTA), and aflatoxin B1 (AFB1). In these experiments, two different methods were used.

In the first method, both MIP and NIP electrodes were subjected under rebinding process in the solution of patulin, 5-HMF, AOH, OTA, and AFB1, separately, and corresponding Δi values were recorded. Next, the imprinting factor
I F =
Δ i
M I P
Δ i
N I P
was calculated. As it is evident in Fig. 6 A, the IF for patulin is about 6.4, 11.4, 11.8, and 13.2 times higher compared to 5-HMF, AOH, OTA, and AFB1, respectively.

In the second method, the analytical signal of 1.0 ng mL−1 patulin in the presence of different concentrations of aforesaid interferents was investigated by designed MIP electrode. As shown in Fig. 6B, the Δi was changed less than 5% in the presence of 50-fold excess of AOH, OTA, and AFB1 and 10-fold excess of 5-HMF. As can be seen, among the investigated compounds, the influence of 5-HMF was slightly greater, due to its structural similarity to patulin. These results indicated that the chemical structure of the patulin molecules was successfully transferred on the polymeric network and the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE is able to preferentially detect the patulin molecules among other compounds.

To assess the reproducibility of the fabricated sensor, six consecutive measurements were performed for determining the concentration of 0.1 and 1.0 ng mL−1 patulin based on the Δi, by using six different electrodes which were independently constructed under the same conditions. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of Δi was 3.49 % for 0.1 ng mL-1 and 3.17 % for 1.0 ng mL-1. Moreover, the MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE presented good stability. After storage at room temperature for 4 weeks, the current response of the modified electrode retained 96.8 % of its original response value. After 6 weeks it still retained 92.1 % of the original value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of patulin in control samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability and efficiency of the fabricated sensor in determination of apple juice quality was investigated by analysis apple juice sample that was purchased from a local supermarkets (Mashhad, Iran). A series of 1 mL apple juice sample were transferred in to a 10 mL volumetric flask and were spiked with different concentration of patulin standard (1 mL) and then diluted by PBS (0.1 mol L−1, pH 6.0) to give a working concentration (0.005, 0.05, 0.5, 5.0 and 50.0 ng mL−1). These samples were analyzed by newly developed MIP sensor based on the standard addition method. The obtained recoveries (97.6 %–99.4 %) and RSD values (1.23 %–4.61 %) (n = 3) prove the potential application of MIP/Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor for detection of patulin in apple juices (Table 2 ).

Sensor performance was also evaluated compared to HPLC method based on the AOAC Official Method 995.10 [56]. For this purpose, two apple concentrate samples were examined by current method and the obtained results were confirmed by an in-parallel HPLC method. For HPLC analysis, patulin was determined by reversed-phase LC (C18 column) with UV detection at 254 nm. Other features of the HPLC analysis were: constant volume of each injection: 5

0 μL, mobile phase: 8% acetonitrile in water and retention time: 7 min. According to the One sample t-test, no significant difference was found between two methods (P < 0.05) (Tables S5, S6, S7). Therefore the designed sensor can be used to analyze apple juice samples with acceptable accuracy.

This work describes the development of a novel procedure for reliable quantification of patulin based on the combination of Au@Cu-MOF/N-GQDs with molecularly imprinted technique. The introduction of N-GQDs on the surface of the electrode effectively improved the conductivity of the sensing system. The unique structure of Au@Cu-MOF effectively increased the number of binding sites on the polymeric network as well as its reduction peak was served as response signal. In order to save experimental time and cost, various factors influencing the preparation of the MIP layer were optimized by experimental design, based on a standard orthogonal array. The construction steps of the sensor were characterized by electrochemical studies. The as prepared sensor presented the low LOD of 0.0007 ng mL−1 and a high sensitivity with a linear range of 0.001–70.0 ng mL−1. The fabricated sensor was practically used to monitor patulin in apple juice samples and the obtained results were confirmed by chromatography method, indicating the proposed strategy could have excellent application prospects in many other MIP-based sensors and biosensors.

Behnaz Hatamluyi: Investigation, Design and perform experiments, Software, Writing - original draft, Formal analysis. Majid Rezayi: Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Hamed Reza Beheshti: Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Mohammad Taher Boroushaki: Supervision, Project administration, Validation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors sincerely thank the TESTA laboratory (Mashhsd, Iran), for kind assisting in the HPLC measurement of patulin samples. This study is the results of a research project and thesis presented for a Post Doc student. This project was financially supported by Vice Chancellor for Research (Grant no. 980734), Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128219.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrodeposition of gold nanoparticles on Cu-based metal-organic framework for the electrochemical detection of nitrite

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Chen, T. Yang, F. Liu, W. Li, Electrodeposition of gold nanoparticles on Cu-based metal-organic framework for the electrochemical detection of nitrite, Sens. Actuators, B. 286 (2019) 401-407.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A layer-by-layer sensing architecture based on dendrimer and ionic liquid supported reduced graphene oxide for simultaneous hollow-fiber solid phase microextraction and electrochemical determination of anti-cancer drug imatinib in biological samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Hatamluyi, Z. Es'haghi, A layer-by-layer sensing architecture based on dendrimer and ionic liquid supported reduced graphene oxide for simultaneous hollow-fiber solid phase microextraction and electrochemical determination of anti-cancer drug imatinib in biological samples, J. Electroanal. Chem. 801 (2017) 439-449.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver nanoparticles decorated polyaniline nanocomposite based electrochemical sensor for the determination of anticancer drug 5-fluorouracil

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.M. Zahed, B. Hatamluyi, F. Lorestani, Z. Es’haghi, Silver nanoparticles decorated polyaniline nanocomposite based electrochemical sensor for the determination of anticancer drug 5-fluorouracil, J. Pharm. Biomed. Anal., 161 (2018) 12-19.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel molecularly imprinted polymer decorated by CQDs@HBNNS nanocomposite and UiO-66-NH2 for ultra-selective electrochemical sensing of oxaliplatin in biological samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Hatamluyi, A. Hashemzadeh, M. Darroudi, A novel molecularly imprinted polymer decorated by CQDs@HBNNS nanocomposite and UiO-66-NH2 for ultra-selective electrochemical sensing of Oxaliplatin in biological samples, Sens. Actuators, B. 307 (2020) 127614-127624.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Screening and development of DNA aptamers as capture probes for colorimetric detection of patulin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Wu, N. Duan, W. Zhang, S. Zhao, Z. Wang, Screening and development of DNA aptamers as capture probes for colorimetric detection of patulin, Anal. Biochem. 508 (2016) 58-64.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface molecularly imprinted polymer capped Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots as a phosphorescent nanosensor for detecting patulin in apple juice

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Zhang, Y. Han, X. Chen, X. Luo, J. Wang, T. Yue, et al., Surface molecularly imprinted polymer capped Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots as a phosphorescent nanosensor for detecting patulin in apple juice, Food Chem. 232 (2017) 145-154.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mimetic Ag nanoparticle/Zn-based MOF nanocomposite (AgNPs@ZnMOF) capped with molecularly imprinted polymer for the selective detection of patulin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Bagheri, A. Khataee, B. Habibi, J. Hassanzadeh, Mimetic Ag nanoparticle/Zn-based MOF nanocomposite (AgNPs@ZnMOF) capped with molecularly imprinted polymer for the selective detection of patulin, Talanta. 179 (2018) 710-718.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An ultrasensitive aptasensor based on fluorescent resonant energy transfer and exonuclease-assisted target recycling for patulin detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wu, E. Xu, Z. Jin, J. Irudayaraj, An ultrasensitive aptasensor based on fluorescent resonant energy transfer and exonuclease-assisted target recycling for patulin detection, Food Chem. 249 (2018) 136-142.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of patulin in apple juice by liquid chromatography: collaborative study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.R. Brause, M.W. Trucksess, F.S. Thomas, S.W. Page, Determination of patulin in apple juice by liquid chromatography: collaborative study, J. AOAC Int. 79 (1996) 451-455.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Behnaz Hatamluyi, a postdoctoral researcher in Dept. of Pharmacology, Faculty of Medicine, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran. Her research interests include the development of electrochemical sensors and biosensors based on different nanomaterial.

Majid Rezayi, is currently an assistant professor of analytical chemistry in Faculty of Medicine at Mashhad University of Medical Sciences (Iran). He completed his PhD in University of Putra Malaysia (Malaysia) in 2011. This was followed by a period of four years of postdoctoral research at National University of Malaysia and University of Malaya, respectively. His research interests are in the areas of synthesis and charcterization of nanomaterials and biosensors. He is particularly interested in applying interdisciplinary and integrative approaches to study biomedical problems.

Hamed Reza Beheshti, has received his Pharm. D., degree from School of Pharmacy, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran. He is Managing Director and Technical Officer of Testa Quality Control Lab.

Mohammad Taher Boroushaki, a Professor of Toxicology in Dept. of Pharmacology, Faculty of Medicine, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran. He has been teaching pharmacology and toxicology (theory and experimental) to the students of medicine, dentistry, pharmacy and nursing, since 1989 and also to the postgraduate students, since 2001. He has received his Pharm. D., degree in 1988 from School of Pharmacy, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran and Ph. D., degree in 2001 from Molecular Toxicology Group, Faculty of Biomedical & Life Sciences. University of Surrey. Guildford, UK. His research interests are in the fields of nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, medicinal plants, antioxidants and diabetes.
